concepts.title: Harmonic Progression and its expression
concepts.conceptBody:
Harmonic Progression (H.P.) 

The sequence ${{a}_{1}},{{a}_{2}},\ldots ..{{a}_{n}}$ is said to be a H.P. if $\frac{1}{{{a}_{1}}},\frac{1}{{{a}_{2}}},\frac{1}{{{a}_{3}}}\ldots \ldots .\frac{1}{{{a}_{n}}}$ is an A.P.

The ${{n}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term of a H.P. $\left( {{t}_{n}} \right)$ is given by ${{t}_{n}}=\frac{1}{a+\left( n-1 \right)d}$ where $a=\frac{1}{{{a}_{1}}}$ and $d=\frac{1}{{{a}_{2}}}-\frac{1}{{{a}_{1}}}$

concepts.title: Solved Problems on Harmonic Progression
concepts.conceptBody:
Solved Problems on Harmonic Progression

Q) If the ${{(m+1)}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}},{{(n+1)}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ and ${{(r+1)}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term of an A.P. are in G.P. $m,n,r$ are in H.P., Show that ratio of the common difference to the first term in the AP is $\left( -2/n \right)$. 

Solution : 

Let ' $a$ ' be the first term and ' $d$ ' be common difference of the AP. Let $x,y,z$ be the ${{(m+1)}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}},{{(n+1)}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ and ${{(r+1)}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term of the A.P. then $x=a+md,y=a+nd,z=a+rd$. Since $x,y,z$ are in G.P.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{y}^{2}}=xz$ i.e. ${{(a+nd)}^{2}}=\left( a+rd \right)\left( a+md \right)$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{d}}{\text{a}}=\frac{\text{r}+\text{m}-2\text{n}}{{{\text{n}}^{2}}-\text{rm}}$

Now, m, n, $r$ in H.P.

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{2}{\text{n}}=\frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}}+\frac{1}{\text{r}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{2}{\text{n}}=\frac{\text{m}+\text{r}}{\text{mr}}$

Hence $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{d}}{\text{a}}=\frac{2\left( \frac{\text{r}+\text{m}}{2}-\text{n} \right)}{\text{n}\left( \text{n}-\frac{\text{rm}}{\text{n}} \right)}$

$\frac{2\left( \frac{\text{mr}}{\text{n}}-\text{n} \right)}{\text{n}\left( \text{n}-\frac{\text{rm}}{\text{n}} \right)}=-\frac{2}{\text{n}}$

Q) If $a,b,c$ are respectively ${{p}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}},{{q}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$, and ${{r}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ terms of H.P., prove that 

$\left( q-r \right)+ca\left( r-p \right)+ab\left( p-q \right)=0$

Solution : 

$\text{A}$ and $\text{D}$ be the first term and common difference of the AP formed by the reciprocals of the given H.P.

$\frac{1}{a}=A+(p-1) D$ (i)

$\frac{1}{b}=A+(q-1) D$ (ii)

$\frac{1}{c}=A+(r-1) D$ (iii)

Subtracting (iii) from (ii) we get $\frac{c-b}{bc}=\left( q-r \right)D$

$\Rightarrow$ $b c(q-r)$ $=-\frac{(b-c)}{D}$

L.H.S $=\sum b c(q-r)$

$=-\sum \frac{\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{D}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{D}} \sum(\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c})$

$=\frac{1}{\mathrm{D}}[\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{c}-\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b}]=0=$ R.H.S.

Q) If $\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{c}+\frac{1}{a-b}+\frac{1}{c-b}=0$, prove that $a,b,c$ are in H.P., unless $b=a+c$ 

Solution : 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{c}+\frac{1}{a-b}+\frac{1}{c-b}=0 \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{a+c}{ac}+\frac{a+c-2b}{ac-b\left( a+c \right)+{{b}^{2}}}=0 \\ \end{array}$

Let $\text{a}+\text{c}=\text{t}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{t}}{\text{ac}}+\frac{\text{t}-2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}}{\text{ac}-\text{bt}+{{\text{b}}^{2}}}=0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }act-b{{t}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}t+act-2abc=0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}{{\text{t}}^{2}}-{{\text{b}}^{2}}\text{t}-2\text{act}+2\text{bac}=0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ bt $\left( \text{t}-\text{b} \right)-2\text{ac}\left( \text{t}-\text{b} \right)=0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \text{t}-\text{b} \right)\left( \text{bt}-2\text{ac} \right)=0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ t}=\text{b}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }a+c=b\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }b\left( a+c \right)2ac$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }a+c=b\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }b=\frac{2ac}{a+c}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ a, b, c are in H.P. or $a+c=b$
